concept: Mouth / Speaking-Eating Gate / The Threshold of Exchange
description: The noun naming the opening through which we eat, drink, breathe, and speak—the threshold between inner body and outer world. In Phi, mouth is understood as the gate of exchange, where nourishment enters and words leave, where breath flows both ways. This word names both the physical feature and the function of verbal expression, of taste, of the intimate boundary where self meets world.
sound_symbolism: The word opens with 'phu', where the gentle bilabial fricative 'ph' enacts the lips themselves—soft boundaries that open and close, that shape breath into speech. The deep 'u' holds the cavity within. This flows through 'la', where the liquid carries the act of speaking or swallowing—words and food flowing through the opening. The final 'e' brings clarity, the precision of articulation, words formed and released. The arc traces mouth's function: soft boundary, flowing exchange, clear articulation.
grammatical_notes: Functions as a noun referring to the mouth specifically. Often appears in expressions related to eating, speaking, taste, and expression. Distinguished from specific parts (lips, tongue, teeth) as the whole functional unit.

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: Right speech emerges through the mouth; mindful eating enters through the mouth. 'Phulae' names the gate where these fundamental practices of consciousness occur.
pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: What leaves our mouth shapes the world. 'Phulae' invites awareness of the mouth as threshold of responsibility—guardian of what we say.

tags.physical: the body opening for eating and speaking
tags.dialogue: the gate through which speech emerges
